reference: GATE CS — Theory of Computation / Automata

map.condition: Converting NFA to DFA — upper bound on states
DFA for language divisible by k — number of states
map.use: |Q| = k \text{ states (one per residue class mod } k\text{)}

map.condition: Combining two DFAs with m and n states via product construction

formulas.name: Subset Construction (NFA to DFA)
formulas.plain: |Q_DFA| <= 2^|Q_NFA|
formulas.whenToUse: Upper-bounding states when converting an NFA with n states to an equivalent DFA.

terms.means: set of states in the NFA
set of states in the resulting DFA (subsets of Q_NFA)

formulas.trap: The bound 2^n is the WORST case; in practice far fewer subsets are reachable. GATE questions often ask for the minimum DFA, not the naive subset-construction DFA.

formulas.name: NFA–DFA Language Equivalence
formulas.latex: L(NFA) = L(DFA) \text{ — every NFA has an equivalent DFA}
formulas.plain: L(NFA) = L(DFA)  [for every NFA there exists an equivalent DFA]
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether determinism restricts recognizing power for finite automata.

terms.means: language accepted by machine M

formulas.trap: This equivalence holds for FA but NOT for PDA: non-deterministic PDA is strictly more powerful than deterministic PDA.

formulas.name: Epsilon-Closure Definition
formulas.plain: epsilon-closure(q) = {q} union epsilon-closure of all states reachable from q on epsilon
formulas.whenToUse: Converting an NFA-epsilon to a plain NFA or DFA — must fold epsilon transitions first.

terms.means: a state in the NFA
set of states reachable from q on epsilon transition

formulas.trap: Every state is in its own epsilon-closure (reflexive). Forgetting q itself is a common error.

formulas.name: NFA Acceptance Condition
formulas.latex: w \in L(NFA) \iff \exists \text{ a computation path that ends in } F
formulas.plain: w is accepted if at least one path through the NFA on w ends in an accept state
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding membership in an NFA-recognized language; contrasted with DFA where ALL paths are deterministic.

terms.means: set of accept states
input string

formulas.trap: NFA accepts if ANY branch accepts; rejection requires ALL branches to fail.

formulas.name: Myhill–Nerode Theorem
formulas.latex: \text{min states of DFA for } L = \text{number of equivalence classes of } \equiv_L
formulas.plain: minimum DFA states = number of Myhill-Nerode equivalence classes of L
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a language is not regular (infinite classes) or counting minimum states.

terms.means: Myhill-Nerode equivalence: x equiv_L y iff for all z, xz in L <=> yz in L

formulas.trap: The theorem works on the language, not a particular DFA — it gives the ABSOLUTE minimum, not just a locally reduced machine.

formulas.name: Table-Filling (Distinguishability) Algorithm
formulas.latex: \text{Mark } (p,q) \text{ if one} \in F,\ \text{one} \notin F;\text{ then propagate: mark }(p,q)\text{ if }(\delta(p,a),\delta(q,a))\text{ marked}
formulas.plain: Mark (p,q) distinguishable if one is final and other is not; propagate for each symbol a
formulas.whenToUse: Minimizing a given DFA by merging indistinguishable (equivalent) states.

terms.means: transition from state p on symbol a
pair of states being tested for distinguishability

formulas.trap: States that are both non-final can still be distinguishable; don't merge them without checking all transitions.

formulas.name: Minimal DFA from NDFA — NOT Always 2^n
formulas.plain: Minimal DFA from n-state NFA has at most 2^n states but can have far fewer
formulas.whenToUse: Answering TRUE/FALSE: 'Minimal DFA for an n-state NDFA always has 2^n states' — the answer is FALSE.

terms.means: number of states in the NFA

formulas.trap: GATE classic trap: the statement 'always 2^n' is FALSE. The worst-case is 2^n, but the minimum DFA can be much smaller.

formulas.name: Product Construction for Intersection / Union
formulas.plain: States of product DFA = |Q_M1| * |Q_M2|
formulas.whenToUse: Building a DFA for L1 intersection L2 or L1 union L2 when DFAs for L1 (m states) and L2 (n states) are given.

terms.means: number of states in DFA M_i

formulas.trap: The product construction gives an upper bound; after minimization the actual state count may be smaller.

formulas.name: DFA for Divisibility by k
formulas.plain: |Q| = k states, one per residue class mod k
formulas.whenToUse: Counting states of the minimum DFA that accepts strings whose numeric value (or length, or product) is divisible by k.

terms.means: the divisor
states, labelled by remainder 0 through k-1

formulas.trap: For a product-mod-7 language over {1,2,3,4}, only residues actually reachable from residue 1 (initial) need to be counted — may be fewer than k states.

formulas.name: DFA for Combined Divisibility Conditions
formulas.latex: |Q| = k_1 \times k_2 \quad (\text{one state per pair of residues})
formulas.plain: |Q| = k1 * k2 states for language where count_a mod k1 = 0 AND count_b mod k2 = 0
formulas.whenToUse: Minimum DFA for strings where #a mod k1 = 0 AND #b mod k2 = 0 simultaneously.

terms.means: divisor for occurrences of symbol a
divisor for occurrences of symbol b

formulas.trap: k1*k2 is the maximum; after checking which pairs are reachable the minimum may differ.

formulas.name: DFA for 'k-th Symbol from Right'
formulas.plain: DFA for '3rd symbol from right is 1' needs 2^3 = 8 states; NFA needs 4 states
formulas.whenToUse: Any problem asking for the minimum DFA recognizing 'the k-th symbol from right is X'.

terms.means: position from the right end

formulas.trap: The NFA needs only k+1 states but the equivalent minimal DFA needs 2^k states — a common GATE question.

formulas.name: Regular Expression Operators
formulas.latex: R_1 + R_2 \;(\text{union}),\quad R_1 R_2 \;(\text{concat}),\quad R^* \;(\text{Kleene star})
formulas.plain: R1 + R2 (union), R1 R2 (concatenation), R* (Kleene star / zero or more)
formulas.whenToUse: Writing or interpreting any regular expression over an alphabet.

terms.means: zero or more repetitions of R, includes epsilon
one or more repetitions of R; R+ = RR*

formulas.trap: R* includes the empty string epsilon; R+ does not. Confusing the two changes the language.

formulas.name: Kleene Star Identities
formulas.plain: (R*)* = R*,  empty_set* = {epsilon},  epsilon* = {epsilon}
formulas.whenToUse: Simplifying regular expressions; recognizing edge cases in GATE option elimination.

terms.means: empty language (no strings)
empty string

formulas.trap: emptyset* is NOT empty — it equals {epsilon}. This is the most common GATE MCQ trap in this area.

formulas.name: Thompson's Construction (Regex to NFA)
formulas.latex: \text{For } R_1 R_2: \text{chain NFAs; for } R_1+R_2: \text{add start/accept with } \varepsilon\text{-moves; for } R^*: \text{add loop}
formulas.plain: Thompson's: union adds epsilon-branching start; concat chains; star adds epsilon loop back to start
formulas.whenToUse: Mechanically converting a regular expression to an NFA.

terms.means: epsilon transition (free move without consuming input)

formulas.trap: Thompson's NFA always has exactly one start and one accept state; do not merge intermediate accept states.

formulas.name: Complement of Regular Language
formulas.latex: \overline{L} = \Sigma^* \setminus L \text{ — swap final and non-final states in the MIN-DFA}
formulas.plain: complement(L) = swap final/non-final states of the MINIMIZED DFA of L
formulas.whenToUse: When asked to build DFA or regex for the complement of a regular language.

terms.means: complement of final states: Q \ F

formulas.trap: Complement swap must be done on a COMPLETE DFA (dead/trap state must exist); otherwise unreachable non-final states become spurious accept states.

formulas.name: Regex for Proper Substrings of a Fixed String
formulas.latex: \text{Proper non-null substrings of } s: \text{ enumerate substrings of length } 1 \text{ to } |s|-1 \text{ and union}
formulas.plain: List all proper non-null substrings explicitly and write their union as a regular expression
formulas.whenToUse: GATE problem: 'give a regex for proper non-null substrings of 0110' — enumerate, then write union.

terms.means: length of the fixed string s

formulas.trap: The null/empty string is usually excluded when 'non-null' is specified. Also, identical substrings need not be repeated in the union.

formulas.name: Closure Properties of Regular Languages
formulas.latex: \text{Regular languages are closed under: } \cup,\ \cap,\ \text{complement},\ \cdot,\ {}^*,\ \text{reversal, homomorphism, inverse-hom}
formulas.plain: Regular languages closed under: union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star, reversal, homomorphism, inverse homomorphism
formulas.whenToUse: Any GATE question asking 'is the result of operation X on regular languages still regular?'

terms.means: union
intersection

formulas.trap: Regular languages are NOT closed under infinite union or intersection of infinitely many languages.

formulas.name: DFA for L-complement (F2 = L^c)
formulas.plain: DFA for complement: same Q, delta, q0 but final states = Q minus F
formulas.whenToUse: Building the DFA for the complement language from a given DFA for L.

terms.means: all states that were NOT accepting in the original DFA

formulas.trap: Requires the DFA to be COMPLETE (total transition function). Add a dead/sink state if any transitions are missing.

formulas.name: Mealy Machine Definition
formulas.plain: Mealy: output depends on current state AND current input symbol
formulas.whenToUse: FSM problems where the output is given in the transition table as a function of (state, input).

terms.means: output alphabet
output function: (state, input) -> output symbol

formulas.trap: In a Mealy machine the output is on TRANSITIONS; in a Moore machine it is on STATES. Mealy can simulate Moore with same or fewer states.

formulas.name: Moore Machine Definition
formulas.plain: Moore: output depends only on the current state (output labelled on states)
formulas.whenToUse: FSM problems where the output column in the state table depends only on the state, not the input.

terms.means: Moore output function maps each state to an output symbol

formulas.trap: A Moore machine may need more states than an equivalent Mealy machine by at most a factor related to the output alphabet.

formulas.name: Non-Determinism: FA vs PDA vs TM
formulas.plain: NFA = DFA (power); NPDA strictly > DPDA; NTM = DTM (same class, different time)
formulas.whenToUse: GATE question: 'Which conversion is NOT possible algorithmically?' Non-det PDA to det PDA is not always possible.

terms.means: equivalent in language recognition power
strictly more powerful

formulas.trap: NFA to DFA conversion IS always possible. NPDA to DPDA is NOT always possible. This is the most tested GATE distinction.
